一.meaning_mnemonic: Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
一.meaning_hint: To remember the meaning of <kanji>One</kanji>, imagine yourself there at the scene of the crime. You grab <kanji>One</kanji> in your arms, trying to prop it up, trying to hear its last words. Instead, it just splatters some blood on your face. "Who did this to you?" you ask. The number One points weakly, and you see number Two running off into an alleyway. He's always been jealous of number One and knows he can be number one now that he's taken the real number one out.
一.reading_mnemonic: As you're sitting there next to <kanji>One</kanji>, holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill One) that causes the person it touches to get extremely <reading>itchy</reading> (いち).
一.reading_hint: Make sure you feel the ridiculously <reading>itchy</reading> sensation covering your body. It climbs from your hands, where you're holding the number <kanji>One</kanji> up, and then goes through your arms, crawls up your neck, goes down your body, and then covers everything. It becomes uncontrollable, and you're scratching everywhere, writhing on the ground. It's so itchy that it's the most painful thing you've ever experienced (you should imagine this vividly, so you remember the reading of this kanji).

人.meaning_mnemonic: Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!
人.meaning_hint: Know the radicals, and you shall know the kanji.
人.reading_mnemonic: Only one <kanji>person</kanji> at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん) complained about the new <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) policy, and that person was you.
人.reading_hint: Imagine how angry you were about the policy. You'll have to come up with something to be angry about yourself (pick something that would actually make you angry, like maybe you're only allowed to wear cut off short-shorts jeans at Nintendo now), but if you pick something good, you'll definitely remember the two readings for this. Just remember you're working at Nintendo, and the policy has to be something about jeans. Then, imagine yourself as the only person complaining and everyone else in their dumb jeans (or lack-thereof) shaking their head at you because you're being a fool.

力.meaning_mnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.
力.meaning_hint: If you know the radical, you should be feeling fine… No, you should feel powerful!
力.reading_mnemonic: Who has the most power of anyone? It's the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers. They are battling their arch enemy <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき). They defeat him and <reading>lock</reading> (りょく) him up, so that he can't hurt anyone ever again.
力.reading_hint: Know anyone named <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき), even a famous person like Ricky Ricardo or Ricky Gervais? Imagine that person's face on the monster the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers are fighting. Now, imagine the Power Rangers <reading>lock</reading>ing (りょく) him up. He looks much less scary once he's locked up. Phew.

三.meaning_mnemonic: This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine <radical>one</radical> and <radical>two</radical> together and what do you get? <kanji>Three</kanji>!
三.meaning_hint: This one should be pretty self explanatory! Even easier, you can look at this kanji and see it has three straight lines. You know what one looks like. You know what two looks like. So... this is three!
三.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Three</kanji> is the number of chances you get with <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん). He has a list, and when you get to three "naughty things" lines, that's it. You're on Santa-san's three strikes and you're out list.
三.reading_hint: Picture Santa-san sitting at his desk at the North Pole, eating cookies, and marking one, two, then finally three lines next to names. Once the third line appears, the kid doesn't get presents anymore.

上.meaning_mnemonic: You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
上.meaning_hint: Focus your imagination on how body parts should be buried, not above ground like this. Sure, finding a toe is a little strange, but finding a toe above the ground? Downright silly.
上.reading_mnemonic: Of course when you find a toe <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう).
上.reading_hint:
Try to remember Joe, the local farmhand. He is a recurring character that will come up anytime the reading for a kanji is じょう.

He is a very big man, and he wears farmer's clothes. It's important to imagine your version of Joe as very big, because it will help us to differentiate him from Jo-Anne, who is the character associated with the similar, but shorter reading (and character) じょ. Although Joe will develop as you go through all the じょう reading kanji, for now, just imagine him as a big, kinda slow dude, who works for a local farmer. Also, he only has nine or fewer toes, apparently.

下.meaning_mnemonic: There's the <radical>ground</radical>, and there's a <radical>toe</radical> just <kanji>below</kanji> it. Yep, you know this means "below" because the ground has a toe below it in this kanji.
下.meaning_hint: Where's that toe again? Below the ground. Maybe it's your toe. Imagine digging it out and thinking, Ugh, why does it have to be under the ground like this? So irritating. Will I be able to sew it back on in time to save it?
下.reading_mnemonic: You keep digging in the ground for the toe <kanji>below</kanji>, but instead you hit something hard and metallic. You try to dig around it, but it's huge. Turns out it's a <reading>car</reading> (か). Keep digging until you unearth the car below. (Inside, you find the toe you were looking for.)
下.reading_hint: There's another useful on'yomi reading that just isn't useful enough for you to learn now. That reading is げ, but we're only going to worry about that much, much later when you have to learn one or two vocab words that read 下 as げ. That's the problem with really common kanji — too many readings.

大.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
大.meaning_hint: So, if you know the radical, you know the meaning of this kanji as well.
大.reading_mnemonic: There's this big guy (that's how you remember the radical, remember?), and he's wearing a huge <reading>Tie Dye</reading> (たい, だい) shirt. Because he's so huge, the tie dye shirt is also super huge. Everything about him is pretty big, but the tie dye stands out more than anything.
大.reading_hint: When you look at the big guy in your head, imagine him covered in tie dye head to toe. Think about how ridiculous it looks on him, especially since he's such a big guy. Shirt. Sweatpants. Hat. Shoes... it goes on and on.

天.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>heaven</radical> is the same as the kanji <kanji>heaven</kanji>, making this an easy kanji to memorize.
天.meaning_hint: If you know your radicals well (and you should!) you know the meaning of this kanji well, as well!
天.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Heaven</kanji>... nowadays, people aren't all that good. Things have gotten bad. That's why the acceptance rate to get into heaven is only <reading>ten</reading> (てん) percent!
天.reading_hint: Imagine yourself in line to get into heaven. You're super worried, because you're not sure if you'll get in. You heard there was a one in <reading>ten</reading> chance... those aren't good odds. Feel nervous in your imagination. Feel worried. Feel the pressure as you get closer and closer to the gate... will you make it?? Ten percent!

四.meaning_mnemonic: Imagine contorting yourself in such a way that your <radical>mouth</radical> now has your <radical>legs</radical> inside it. You've bent your body in all the wrong ways to achieve this. In fact, if you look at yourself, you look like a square now... You're just a person with <kanji>four</kanji> sides.
四.meaning_hint: If you can see this kanji as a kind of square, you should be able to remember it means "four" as well, since squares always have four sides.
四.reading_mnemonic: Now that you have <kanji>four</kanji> sides, you look like a four-sided animal: the <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Look at yourself in the mirror and see an adorable four-sided sheep looking back at you. Imagine yourself with perfectly square wool. (You can only see four sides because mirrors aren't 3D. So you're a flat, cartoonish sheep.)
四.reading_hint: What's better than a four-sided sheep?

目.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.
目.meaning_hint: Well... you'll know them both as long as you studied your radicals, young grasshopper. If you didn't, then you're just a young grasshopper who needs to study their radicals.
目.reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>eyes</kanji> are important to you, right? Sadly, one of your eyes is missing right now, because a sheep grabbed it and pulled it out, and is now running away from you, yelling his sheep call.... "<reading>Mehhhhhh</reading> (め)!"
目.reading_hint: Of course, feel the pain of your eye being gone... and struggle to get it back. You eventually give up because the sheep is too fast, but then you come up with an idea. What if I call out back to the sheep in its own language? Go ahead and cover one eye and start making "MEHHH" noises to see if you can get the sheep to come back to you and give you your eye. It's up to you whether it works or not, but be sure to feel frustrated by its reaction, or happy by it. Either one works.

分.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.
分.meaning_hint: Picture the fins falling onto the sword, and being divided into many parts. And if those fins represented hours, they’d be divided into minutes. Makes sense, right?
分.reading_mnemonic: Now that the fins have been cut into neat <kanji>part</kanji>s, it's time to cut something else: <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん).
分.reading_hint: This is how you feed your people: neatly divided, delicious buns. Picture these delicious buns divided into perfectly shaped parts. Yummy.

少.meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>small</radical> <radical>slide</radical>. The thing about small slides is that <kanji>few</kanji> people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down.
少.meaning_hint: Imagine a small slide (how could you not, after you see the radicals?). So few people can use this thing, really, but there are a few little ones who can fit on it at least.
少.reading_mnemonic: Another reason so <kanji>few</kanji> people can use it is that the <reading>Sho</reading>gun (しょう) is trying to go down the slide. We're talking a grown man who's way too big for this little slide, so a few people have to wait behind him — it's a slide traffic jam.
少.reading_hint: Imagine this in your head. Even the few people who fit on the slide are struggling to get past the Shogun. Meanwhile the Japanese secret service is trying to make sure no one gets close to the shogun while he slides. A few people are crying. They want to slide, they want to leave… It's chaos.

引.meaning_mnemonic: If you have a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> you need to <kanji>pull</kanji> the two back together to shoot it. If you can't pull it, you won't be shooting anything.
引.meaning_hint: Imagine taking a stick and pulling it back into your bow. Imagine how hard you have to pull. This thing is really strong!
引.reading_mnemonic: When you <kanji>pull</kanji> the bow back, your body strains and you feel an immense <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) rise inside of you. You keep pulling and the heat grows hotter and hotter.
引.reading_hint: Imagine using all your strength to pull on this bow. Imagine the heat rising in your body. There's sweat dripping down your face. It's so hot, but you have to keep pulling!

外.meaning_mnemonic: You're known to get the ailment <radical>evening</radical> <radical>toe</radical>, which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go <kanji>outside</kanji>.
外.meaning_hint: After imagining your evening toe overheating, rush outside to fix it. It's the best way to prevent fire toe, which is way worse. In the evening it's nice and cold outside too, so feel this on your skin, and feel the cold air on your toe, which is starting to get some relief.
外.reading_mnemonic: You go <kanji>outside</kanji> (you're not supposed to be out there at night!) and you're accosted by a couple of guys. Then, all of a sudden, <reading>Guy</reading> (がい) Fawkes jumps from the roof above you and takes them out, then starts running. "Guy Fawkes!" you yell after him!
外.reading_hint: If you don't know Guy Fawkes, you should do a little reading up on him to make this mnemonic make sense. Also, Anonymous People wear a Guy Fawkes mask, if that helps any.

平.meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>dry</radical> your <radical>horns</radical> you need to lay them <kanji>flat</kanji>. If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's <kanji>peace</kanji>.
平.meaning_hint: Imagine carefully taking off your horns and laying them flat on a paper towel. You have to smooth them out so they're as flat as possible.
平.reading_mnemonic: Being <kanji>flat</kanji> isn't enough, so you cover the whole thing in <reading>hay</reading> (へい). Hay absorbs water, so you add a flat layer of it.
平.reading_hint: Imagine carefully laying a flat layer of hay on top of your flat horns. You feel really at peace and... you're tired now. You decided to lie on top of the hay and take a nap while you wait for your horns to dry.

糸.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
糸.meaning_hint: This is why it's good to make sure you know your radicals.
糸.reading_mnemonic: You have some <kanji>thread</kanji>... what's the first thing you do with it? You take it and put it in your mouth, and start sucking it down like spaghetti. You have decided to <reading>eat</reading> (いと) the thread.
糸.reading_hint: Think about how it tastes. Think about how it feels in your throat. Let it get caught on your teeth and around your tongue. It's a weird feeling having thread in your mouth, and you want to use that weird feeling to remember you're going to <reading>eat</reading> it.

見.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
見.meaning_hint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. If you do, you'll know a lot of kanji as well, almost magically!
見.reading_mnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"
見.reading_hint: The struggling to see bit is pretty important — make sure that you slowly see a little better and better as the person comes closer, but it's difficult the whole time (struggles are good for memory). Then, it starts to become more and more clear, and you start saying "that can't be <reading>me</reading>. No way it's me..." When the person comes up to you (the person, being you) you learn about parallel universes and other fabulous things. Still, the whole time you can't stop thinking about how "this person looks just like me... no, this person is me!"

回.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.
回.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself counting and counting smaller and smaller mouths inside of each other, recording the number of times you can find a mouth inside another mouth. Dig deeper and deeper until it feels ridiculous how small the mouths are getting. Be sure to focus on the counting and the number of times you find one. Now, how many mouths is this times two, because your mouth is like this too.
回.reading_mnemonic: If you're having a hard time wrapping your head around the infinite number of <kanji>times</kanji> these mouths <kanji>revolve</kanji>, just picture yourself in a little <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) going around and down through the mouths.
回.reading_hint: Your kayak is slashing and dipping through the revolving mouths, going down and down more times than you can count!

当.meaning_mnemonic: What superhero is most like a <radical>triceratops</radical>? …If you said <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you're <kanji>correct</kanji>! Why is that the <kanji>right</kanji> answer? Because the triceratops has three horns, and Wolverine has three blades coming out of his hands. It's obvious, isn't it? But you knew that of course.
当.meaning_hint: Picture a triceratops. Now picture Wolverine. An equals sign between them. A little arrow pointing at the horns, an arrow pointing at the blades. And a big flashing CORRECT sign above them. Need I say more?
当.reading_mnemonic: Because you got the answer <kanji>correct</kanji>, you win a free trip to <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Congrats!
当.reading_hint: If you're lucky, you might see Wolverine fighting a triceratops in the middle of Shibuya Crossing in Tokyo. Heroes and monsters are always fighting in Tokyo, right? At least that's what my anime tells me.

体.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>book</radical> all about the human <kanji>body</kanji>. Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.
体.meaning_hint: Imagine looking down at your body and thinking, huh, I wonder what all that junk is? Oh well, I'm sure the leader has it all figured out in that book of hers.
体.reading_mnemonic: The leader's <kanji>body</kanji> book also taught her about something very body specific: how to tie a <reading>tie</reading> (たい). You can't have a tie on your body, no no. Those are for the leader and her smart body.
体.reading_hint: Picture the leader, reading a book, putting a tie on her body. Look around at you and your family and your neighbors. You don't know what your body meat-bags do and you don't know how to tie a tie. Womp womp.

空.meaning_mnemonic: What was the biggest <radical>hole</radical> in the <radical>construction</radical> of the world? The <kanji>sky</kanji>. If you think about it, the sky is like a big hole someone forgot to fill in. That's why this kanji also means <kanji>empty</kanji>! Makes sense — nothing is more empty than the sky.
空.meaning_hint: Next time you look up at the sky, try to imagine it's a big empty hole you're stuck at the bottom of.
空.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>sky</kanji> is usually empty until clouds stage a <reading>coup</reading> (くう). Basically you can think of the sky as the site for one coup after another as the weather changes.
空.reading_hint: First the sky's cloudy, then it's clear and empty, then overcast again... Just coup after coup!

化.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> with a <radical>spoon</radical> is a powerful thing. Even something as ordinary as a spoon can create great <kanji>change</kanji> in the world when given to the right person.
化.meaning_hint: Imagine your leader-hero holding a spoon out, then doing something awesome with it to totally change everything for the better. It's amazing the change this person can bring about with such a simple, everyday object!
化.reading_mnemonic: Then, this hero of yours takes their spoon and says some magic words into it, <kanji>changing</kanji> it into a <reading>car</reading> (か).
化.reading_hint: Of course, doing something like this is utterly amazing. Feel amazed in your imagination, because if you don't, then it'll be hard to remember the reading for this kanji. If you are amazed, you'll know that spoons going to cars = the reading is か, so make sure you take the time to be amazed.

死.meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>yakuza</radical> hands you a <radical>spoon</radical>, <kanji>death</kanji> is coming for you. It's the death spoon, a sign that the yakuza is coming to cause your death.
死.meaning_hint: This spoon isn't for pudding, it's for death!
死.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Death</kanji> comes in the form of a <reading>shee</reading>p (し). The death sheep works for the yakuza, and kills anyone who has their spoon.
死.reading_hint: Picture the death sheep. He looks like a black shadow, and he has a scar on his face. He's holding a bunch of spoons, from all the other people the yakuza marked for death!

知.meaning_mnemonic: You take an <radical>arrow</radical> to the <radical>mouth</radical> because you <kanji>know</kanji> too much. You know some very sensitive information, and someone's sent a bow-slinging enforcer to pin your big blabbermouth shut with arrows.
知.meaning_hint: Think of all the important things you know that you were hoping to blab about: JFK's real killer, the truth about Area 51, Koichi's shocking connections to the Illuminati. You better hurry and get this arrow out of your mouth so you can tell the world what you know!
知.reading_mnemonic: One of the things you <kanji>know</kanji> too much about is the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) plan for world domination! Yes, you know that sheep are in fact plotting to overthrow humanity and take over the world.
知.reading_hint: Take a closer look at that bow-slinging thug who fired the arrow into your mouth. Their shadowy silhouette looks awfully… woolly, doesn't it? It's undeniable: the sheep are trying to shut you up!

思.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>rice paddy</radical> you find a <radical>heart</radical> and it makes you <kanji>think</kanji>. You think a lot of <kanji>thought</kanji>s like, "Whose heart is this? Why is it in a rice paddy? Why am I thinking out loud?"
思.meaning_hint: Think, think, think. Think about this rice paddy heart.
思.reading_mnemonic: As you are <kanji>think</kanji>ing these <kanji>thought</kanji>s, you spot someone on the edge of the rice field. He really reminds you of someone. But who? That's it! He reminds you of that bartender on The Simpsons. What's his name? You think some more. What is it? <reading>Oh! Moe</reading> (おも) is his name!
思.reading_hint: If you don't know Moe from the Simpsons use a different Moe, perhaps the one from the Three Stooges? You should be able to come up with one. Think really hard about the Moe you know best and imagine them standing watching you from the edge of the rice field.

付.meaning_mnemonic: When you take your <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>measurement</radical>s, you realize they're too short, so you <kanji>attach</kanji> things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.
付.meaning_hint: Imagine guiding your leader's dainty feet into the heeled shoes, then attaching the weird head replica. Now picture yourself trying to keep a straight face as you tell them they totally look tall enough.
付.reading_mnemonic: You have to remember the kun'yomi reading of this kanji, and the part you need to remember is つ. So, think of how you would <kanji>attach</kanji> something to something else. What would you need? You'd need <reading>two</reading> (つ) things to put together!
付.reading_hint: Just focus on how attaching requires two things to remember this reading.

身.meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>self</radical> goes down a <radical>slide</radical> and hits a <radical>barb</radical>, it's a painful reminder that you have a <kanji>body</kanji>. Sometimes people think their self is just some kind of floating, disembodied consciousness, but getting a barb stuck in <kanji>oneself</kanji> while going down a slide will quickly remind one of one's bodily existence.
身.meaning_hint: Imagine hurtling down a slide, your inner self reveling in the joy of the moment. Then, you hit a sharp barb — OUCH!!! Suddenly, you're very conscious of your body again.
身.reading_mnemonic: What part of your <kanji>body</kanji> did the barb get stuck in? Your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). That pointy little barb dug deep into your shin and made you painfully aware of the fact that <kanji>oneself</kanji> and one's body are inextricable.
身.reading_hint: Feel the sharp pain in your shin as it radiates through your body. It kinda sucks, but in a way it's reassuring proof of the physical existence of oneself.

道.meaning_mnemonic: Aim the <radical>scooter</radical>'s <radical>neck</radical> at the <kanji>road</kanji>! Scooters are only meant for driving on <kanji>street</kanji>s and <kanji>path</kanji>s, and if you go the wrong <kanji>way</kanji>, you'll probably break your own neck!
道.meaning_hint: Imagine riding your scooter down the road. You're carefully aiming the scooter's neck the right way so you don't end up going out of control and dying.
道.reading_mnemonic:
To remember the reading どう we use the word "doughnut."

You're traveling down this <kanji>road</kanji> so fast that you don't see the giant <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう) that's rolling toward you until the last minute. You crash into it, and both you and the doughnut go tumbling down. Ahg, your neck!
道.reading_hint: Alternate ending: You do a sweet jump, go right through the doughnut hole, take a bite, and land safely back onto the road!

間.meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>gate</radical> that the <radical>sun</radical> goes through at a regular <kanji>interval</kanji>. There is an <kanji>interval of time</kanji> in the sense that the sun goes through it at the same time every day. And there is an <kanji>interval of space</kanji> in the sense that it has to go through the space between the gate posts.
間.meaning_hint: "Interval" is a weird word to remember, but just think of the sun going around the earth and through the gate like clockwork. Every 24 hours it passes through the gate, and each interval is exactly the same.
間.reading_mnemonic:
This method of keeping track of <kanji>interval</kanji>s of time was invented by the great Khans of Mongolia, specifically the most famous Khan of all: Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).

Genghis Khan has had a huge impact on the world as we know it, especially the Eurasian continent of the past, so he's going to come up a lot from now on. Be prepared to get very familiar with him and his friends!
間.reading_hint: Picture Genghis Khan ordering his most skilled inventors and builders to create this special sun gate so they can harness the power of intervals, and all people under his rule can benefit from the accurate telling of time.

定.meaning_mnemonic: You build a <radical>roof</radical> over your <radical>coat rack</radical> because you <kanji>determine</kanji> that this is the best way to protect your coats from the elements.
定.meaning_hint: Think back on all the experiments and calculations you did to determine this. You tested all kinds of things — umbrellas, awnings, tarps — but ultimately you determined that only a roof would suffice.
定.reading_mnemonic: Why did you <kanji>determine</kanji> your coat rack needed a roof? Because your coat pockets are full of <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots! You can't afford to let your tater tots get rained on, so you determined they needed to be properly sheltered with a roof.
定.reading_hint: Stick your hands into your coat pockets and feel those crispy, toasty taters. You're not gonna let them come to any harm.

服.meaning_mnemonic: Take the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>stamp</radical> on the <radical>stool</radical> in front of you and use it on all of your <kanji>clothes</kanji>. Now even if you sell or lose a piece of <kanji>clothing</kanji>, you'll be able to find it again by checking for your special moon stamp.
服.meaning_hint: Imagine placing each piece of clothing you own onto the stool and carefully stamping them with a symbol of the moon. This should stop people from stealing all of your clothes!
服.reading_mnemonic: A few days later you're with a friend when you recognize the <kanji>clothes</kanji> they're wearing. You grab it, rip it off, and scream, "<reading>F**k</reading> (ふく) you!" There is your moon stamp! These are your clothes!!
服.reading_hint: Your friend turns red and tries to pull the clothes back, "F**k you!" they yell. "No, f**k you, these are my clothes and I have proof this time!"

物.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>cow</radical> with <radical>wing</radical>s is flying in the air. What a strange <kanji>thing</kanji>! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.
物.meaning_hint: Imagine if cows could fly. Wouldn't you be jealous of them? I mean, if a cow can fly, any old thing should be able to, right?
物.reading_mnemonic: You want a <kanji>thing</kanji> to help you fly with the cow so you put on your flying <reading>boots</reading> (ぶつ). Your flying boots are a pretty strange thing in themselves, but they're the only way you can keep up.
物.reading_hint: Imagine a thing like flying boots. Do you believe such a thing could be real?

度.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>yurt</radical>'s <radical>stool</radical> is always placed at certain <kanji>degree</kanji>s. The degrees change depending on specific <kanji>occurrence</kanji>s.
度.meaning_hint: Since a yurt is round, the stool has to be angled at certain degrees. It's part of yurt culture. Imagine someone adjusting the stool with a measurement tool to adjust it to an exact degree.
度.reading_mnemonic: Also angled at exact <kanji>degree</kanji>s is the <reading>do</reading>g (ど).
度.reading_hint: The dog changes its degrees at every occurrence of the stool changing. Picture the dog leaning at the exact degree the stool is at.

勝.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> grants a <radical>gladiator</radical> its <radical>power</radical> so he will <kanji>win</kanji>! <kanji>Victory</kanji> will be his!
勝.meaning_hint: Watch as the moon's power fills the gladiator. He starts to glow, grows larger, and has some craters on his face now. Well.. okay.
勝.reading_mnemonic: He <kanji>win</kanji>s over and over again, and soon the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) is even rooting for him. In fact, the Shougun claims him as his personal gladiator of choice.
勝.reading_hint: The Shougun is only interested in people who win. But you probably could have guessed that already.

仮.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> is very <radical>anti</radical>-everything, making it all <kanji>temporary</kanji>. You get a new shirt, "I'm anti shirts!" she yells, and out it goes.
仮.meaning_hint: She tires of things so quickly, you hardly have time to get used to them before they're gone.
仮.reading_mnemonic: The leader's most <kanji>temporary</kanji> possession is her <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Or... cars, because they're all very temporary. She buys one, drives it around, and then suddenly she's anti red car. She needs a blue one!
仮.reading_hint: Picture all of the temporary cars she's tried filling up an entire junkyard. They're all a little different, but in the end they were all just temporary fads.

落.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> are knocked down by a violent <radical>tsunami</radical>, and swirl through the air to <radical>kiss</radical> your face gently as they <kanji>fall</kanji>. With the tsunami came a great windy storm that blew the flowers down. Amongst all the destruction, it's a pretty sight to watch them fall.
落.meaning_hint: Imagine being out in this tsunami-induced storm. You're wet, cold, and scared, but you're calmed and mesmerized by how the flowers dance in the wind before they finally fall.
落.reading_mnemonic: You want to save these flowers that <kanji>fall</kanji> around you, so you take out a drying <reading>rack</reading> (らく) and place them carefully on it. Any flowers that fall will go on the rack like this, so can save them for later.
落.reading_hint: Imagine running around catching the flowers as they fall. Imagine placing each fallen petal onto the rack to keep it safe and dry.

頭.meaning_mnemonic: If you feed a lot of <radical>beans</radical> to a <radical>geoduck</radical>, its <kanji>head</kanji> will grow larger. To a geoduck, beans are a brain food, so by eating beans, it can grow an incredibly large head. Geoducks already look strange enough as it is, let alone with engorged heads.
頭.meaning_hint: Imagine, if you will, a geoduck. Now imagine it with a massive head. It looks ridiculous, and more disgusting than ever. It's actually really creeping you out, because the head keeps getting larger as the geoduck eats more beans. Ew.
頭.reading_mnemonic: As the geoduck eats more and more beans its <kanji>head</kanji> gets bigger and bigger, until finally the head explodes like an <reading>atomi</reading>c (あたま) bomb. BOOM! Geoduck parts go flying everywhere and pieces of its massive head float to the ground as atomic fallout.
頭.reading_hint: Remember how gross geoducks are? Of course you do. Now imagine one exploding its atomic head right in front of you. You've got geoduck mess all over yourself. It's gross, slimy, sticky, and covering you from head to toe. It's wretched.

成.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.
成.meaning_hint: Picture the drunkard standing in front of you. He picked up the sword and is holding it above his head. It's becoming... becoming...
成.reading_mnemonic: The sword has <kanji>become</kanji> a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! It's just like the saber you have! Hey, wait.
成.reading_hint:
If a sword becomes a saber in the hands of a drunkard... then what are you? Geez this became pretty deep.

You slice down the drunkard with your saber and become a little more depressed than you were before.

追.meaning_mnemonic: On a <radical>scooter</radical> there is a <radical>bear</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on its head trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> and <kanji>chase</kanji> you! You accidentally splashed it with water and it's not happy about it. You're on your own scooter, luckily, but that doesn't make this any less terrifying.
追.meaning_hint: Picture the bear on its scooter with the offending drop of water on its head following really closely behind your scooter. You can almost feel his bear breath on your neck!
追.reading_mnemonic: The bear is trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> you because it wants your <reading>o</reading>bi (お) as compensation for its injured pride. The obi is waving in the wind behind your scooter, and the bear is reaching out with its teeth to try and grab it.
追.reading_hint: Picture the bear chasing you on its scooter, trying to reach out and grab the obi off your waist. The drop of water on its head is jiggling from the exertion. Woah, it's getting a little too close!

息.meaning_mnemonic: Imagining one’s <radical>self</radical> without a <radical>heart</radical> can make it hard to draw <kanji>breath</kanji>. Without a heart, you’d have no blood pumping and you would start to die. This would make it increasingly harder to draw breath and get oxygen to stay alive.
息.meaning_hint: Imagine trying to live without a heart. You couldn’t. Your blood stops flowing and your body shuts down. You would no longer be able to draw breath.
息.reading_mnemonic: You know how when people are unconscious and their <kanji>breath</kanji> slows or comes to a stop, they use smelling salts to revive them? Well a smelly <reading>sock</reading> (そく) can work just as well. Socks get really stinky and their stench is powerful enough to revive someone who isn’t drawing breath.
息.reading_hint: Imagine smelling this smelly sock. Imagine the smelly stench of the dirty sock wafting up into your nostrils and filling your lungs with its odor. Gross.

動.meaning_mnemonic: When something is <radical>heavy</radical>, you need a lot of <radical>power</radical> to <kanji>move</kanji> it. It’s not going to move unless you use a lot of power. That thing is heavy and unless you use all the power you got, it’s not moving.
動.meaning_hint: Picture a massive 1 ton weight. It’s heavy. You can’t even begin to move it without some sort of powerful machine to help you. Imagine yourself trying to move it with just your bare hands.
動.reading_mnemonic: Remember that 1 ton weight? Well, actually it’s a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう). A huge, massive, 1 ton doughnut. It’s super heavy and hard to move, but it’s delicious.
動.reading_hint: Okay, so you don’t have enough power to move this doughnut as is. In order to move it, you’re going to have to use your superhuman power of eating. Once you eat enough of it, the remainder will be easy to move. Imagine yourself digging into this massive doughnut and taste its fresh baked goodness on your tongue. It’s mighty tasty.

族.meaning_mnemonic: If someone is headed in your <radical>direction</radical> with a <radical>gun</radical>, nock an <radical>arrow</radical> in your bow and gather your <kanji>tribe</kanji> behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.
族.meaning_hint: This guy headed your direction with his gun doesn’t look friendly at all. Hear the sound of the bowstring as you nock your arrow and prepare your shot to defend yourself. Then imagine the sound of everyone in your tribe doing the same.
族.reading_mnemonic: When he starts to get closer, you notice that this guy is a boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang member. Bosozoku means “running <kanji>tribe</kanji>” and this guy is running right at you. With a gun. So gather your own tribe behind you and prepare to defend yourself against this bosozoku member.
族.reading_hint: You remember what a bosozoku is, right? We wrote about them on Tofugu a while ago. They’re bike gang members and the “zoku” in bosozoku means tribe. Remember that article when you see this kanji.

階.meaning_mnemonic: Look at a <radical>building</radical> and <radical>compare</radical> the <radical>white</radical> parts. Each of those white parts is a <kanji>floor</kanji>. Multi-<kanji>story</kanji> buildings have tons of different floors that you can see from the outside.
階.meaning_hint: All buildings have white marks that you can compare with the rest to see where the floors are.
階.reading_mnemonic: One <kanji>floor</kanji> is just all <reading>kay</reading>aks (かい). You're looking at this building, and every other <kanji>story</kanji> is normal, but that one floor is kayak after kayak after kayak.
階.reading_hint: Kayaks working at desks. Kayaks that are desks. Kayaks walking around checking on other kayaks. The kayak floor is quite odd. It's odd there is a floor only about kayaks.

生.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
生.meaning_hint: Study your radicals and you'll read good.
生.reading_mnemonic: You can save <kanji>life</kanji> or take it away with your new <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?
生.reading_hint: Imagine having someone's life in the palm of your hands. It's up to you whether you protect it with your saber or... well... do the other thing sabers are for.

予.meaning_mnemonic: What does your <radical>mama</radical> tell you to do in the <radical>street</radical>? Look before you cross! You gotta do it <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, or it could be too late.
予.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself about to cross the street, and your mama saying in your ear: "always look beforehand"!
予.reading_mnemonic: As you look <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, you see a <reading>yo-</reading>yo (よ) lying in the street.
予.reading_hint: As you cross the road, you pick up the yo-yo and put it in your pocket. You're really glad you looked beforehand, because you now have a yo-yo!

反.meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>cliff</radical> there's a <radical>stool</radical>. If someone has a stool deep inside a cliff, they are most likely pretty <kanji>anti</kanji>-society, hiding away like that.
反.meaning_hint: Imagine someone who looks super anti-social (and a little feral). Ask them why they're sitting on this stool inside a cliff. They reply it's because they hate people. They're anti-people. In fact, they're anti-everything.
反.reading_mnemonic: Turns out, when you go to this guy and pull off his beard, its the <kanji>anti</kanji>-hero from Star Wars, <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! What is Han doing here, so hidden away? He's obviously hiding from Jabba because he doesn't want to get frozen in carbonite again.
反.reading_hint: Make sure you get to the point where you pull this anti-social guy's beard off, to reveal Han Solo beneath. Be surprised... not just because he's Han Solo, but because he's real, too! Maybe you can yell out "Han! Your people need you!"

感.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>canine</radical> puts its <radical>mouth</radical> on your <radical>heart</radical>, it's a pretty unforgettable <kanji>feeling</kanji>.
感.meaning_hint: The feeling is kind of hard to describe. On the one hand it's warm and loving, but on the other hand it's also kind of wet and slobbery.
感.reading_mnemonic: Overall, though, it's a good <kanji>feeling</kanji> — so good that you feel like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! With this canine lovingly slobbering on your heart, you feel like you could go out and conquer the world!
感.reading_hint: Just be careful not to let in all the Genghis Khan feelings. The confidence and motivation are good, but the feeling of wanting to wantonly slaughter your enemies is probably one you ought to repress.

練.meaning_mnemonic: Pointing a <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> is what you <kanji>practice</kanji> every day. You're incredibly passionate about eastward thread-pointing, so you make sure to practice every day.
練.meaning_hint: You're absolutely obsessed with pointing your thread towards the east, so all you do is practice. Practice, practice, practice.
練.reading_mnemonic: You do all of this <kanji>practice</kanji> to try and afford all of your <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん). One day, all of this practice will pay for all of your rentals.
練.reading_hint: Everything in your life is a rental, so you better get to practicing. Your house, your car, your friends — all of these rentals need to be paid off!

材.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>talent</radical> can become <kanji>lumber</kanji>. Not all trees can do this. Only the talented ones are able to turn themselves into lumber or <kanji>material</kanji>, to be used for a million different things.
材.meaning_hint: Picture a tree standing in the forest. It's thinking so hard you can see the math equations flying around in its branches, until finally a cartoon light bulb appears above its head. This talented tree has figured out how to become lumber!
材.reading_mnemonic: Trees can be turned into all kinds of things, but this one wants to use its <kanji>lumber</kanji> and <kanji>material</kanji> to build a <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい). It's the most difficult instrument to create, so it suits the talent level of this tree!
材.reading_hint: Watch as the tree becomes lumber and that lumber is used to craft a ginormous xylophone. Each key is its own plank of lumber!

残.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Yakuza</radical> <radical>bars</radical> barely exist. The <kanji>remainder</kanji> of them aren't even in Japan.
残.meaning_hint: Imagine a yakuza bar in Zanzibar full of tattooed thugs in colorful swim trunks, all sipping tropical cocktails. No wonder the remainder of the yakuza bars are in Zanzibar — this is a great vibe!
残.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>remainder</kanji> of the yakuza bars aren't in Japan. Strangely, they are all in <reading>Zan</reading>zibar (ざん).
残.reading_hint: Imagine a yakuza bar in Zanzibar. What a strange place to hold out.

的.meaning_mnemonic:
A <radical>white</radical> guy jumps into a <radical>pool</radical> with a <kanji>target</kanji> painted on the bottom. He's aiming for the target, of course.

This kanji can also mean <kanji>-al</kanji>, as in the endings you get on some English adjectives. You know, like the pool is artifici-al. Or painting a target down there is not practic-al. That kind of -al.
的.meaning_hint: Picture this pasty white dude jumping into the target-bottomed pool. The sun glints off of his unnaturally white skin and stings your eyes.
的.reading_mnemonic: This white dude is a huge <reading>techie</reading> (てき) so he has electronic enhancements that make sure he hits the <kanji>target</kanji>. Being a techie has its advantages.
的.reading_hint: He hits the water right on target and the water splashes onto you. Since he was a techie, he was able to perform the perfect dive. And now you’re all wet.

能.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>private</radical> place on the <radical>moon</radical>, you hide two <radical>spoon</radical>s that give you a special <kanji>ability</kanji>. It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!
能.meaning_hint: I wish I had this kind of ability... maybe I'll go up to the moon and try to steal your spoons.
能.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>ability</kanji> to predict the future makes you famous and now everyone knows your name: <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう)! And now that people know about your name and special ability, everyone will love you!
能.reading_hint: Oh, you thought I was talking about that old Nostradamus, who also had the ability to predict things? Nah, you're way cooler than him.

取.meaning_mnemonic: There's an <radical>ear</radical> just sitting on a <radical>stool</radical>. Nobody's watching, so, you <kanji>take</kanji> it.
取.meaning_hint: Feel the rush of stealing something (even though it's a weird something, admittedly). Start walking away quickly. You don't want to get caught!
取.reading_mnemonic: You're the kind of person who likes <vocabulary>to take</vocabulary> things. "What things?" you ask. Little <reading>toe</reading>s (と). Because that isn't creepy.
取.reading_hint: When someone asks you "Do you like to take little toes?" your answer is always "Toetally."

面.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>face</radical> radical and the <kanji>face</kanji> kanji are the same! But this isn't just a face like the one you're looking at your computer/phone with. It's the <kanji>surface</kanji> kind of face too!
面.meaning_hint: The next time you look at your face, also notice how much surface area it has.
面.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>face</kanji> you're looking at, that has a lot of <kanji>surface</kanji> area, belongs to <reading>men</reading> (めん). Not a single man, but all men put together. It's hundreds of thousands of men's faces in a row.
面.reading_hint: Look at the face of all men put together. Measure how much surface area they all take up.

焼.meaning_mnemonic:
In a dangerous <radical>fire</radical>, a <radical>gambler</radical> will always try to <kanji>bake</kanji> something before fleeing to safety. It could be his own house on fire, and the gambler would still use the free heat source to get some baking done. Maybe he's gambling that those baked goods will offset the cost of a new home…

This kanji also means <kanji>burn</kanji>, because that's what happens when you expose something to a lot of heat.
焼.meaning_hint: Imagine you're the gambler. Feel the heat intensify as your nostrils fill with the smell of baked goods.
焼.reading_mnemonic: He used to <kanji>bake</kanji> for the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や). Baseball cakes, bat baguettes, that kind of thing. A career-ending kitchen injury forced him to retire. That's when he developed a gambling problem. But he still likes to bake whenever the Yankees are on TV.
焼.reading_hint: Imagine this guy running around baking in the fire of his home, only this time with a Yankees hat and jersey on. He doesn't bake for them anymore, but he still loves the team.

禁.meaning_mnemonic: Killing two <radical>tree</radical>s with a <radical>jackhammer</radical> is expressly <kanji>prohibit</kanji>ed. Don't get me wrong, you can rip up one tree with your jackhammer, but not two.
禁.meaning_hint: Imagine standing in front of a forest with your jackhammer, staring at a sign with just this kanji on it. Tree plus tree with jackhammer equals prohibited. Hmm.
禁.reading_mnemonic: The person who decided to <kanji>prohibit</kanji> jackhammering two trees is the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. He's the only person who's able to prohibit certain things, so it had to be him.
禁.reading_hint: Picture the King angrily covering his ears as his subjects jackhammer trees. He screams and shouts, his crown bouncing on his head, and points at his new sign that prohibits the jackhammering of trees.

比.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>compare</radical> radical and the <kanji>compare</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!
比.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Compare</kanji> the <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) today with the heat yesterday. Is it hotter today? It's up to you to compare.
比.reading_hint: Feel today's heat. Now compare it to yesterday. You might want to check your thermometer if you're not sure!

防.meaning_mnemonic: If there is a <radical>building</radical> in the <radical>direction</radical> you want to go it will <kanji>prevent</kanji> you from going any further. Like, come on kid, you think you can just walk right through a building? Don't make me laugh!
防.meaning_hint: There's a building right there. Try to go that direction. Oh, did the building prevent you? Told you so.
防.reading_mnemonic: The building is <kanji>prevent</kanji>ing you from going the right direction, so you pull a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball out of your bag, aim, and bowl the building over. The building, and all the ones around it, go tumbling down. But now the rubble is preventing you from going further.
防.reading_hint: Your bowling antics are now preventing a bunch of people from going the directions they want to go. Nice job.

穴.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>hole</radical> radical and the <kanji>hole</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Spectacular!
穴.reading_mnemonic: <reading>Ana</reading> (あな) fell into a <kanji>hole</kanji>. Laugh at Ana and her unfortunate misfortune of having fallen into a hole like a ninny. Haha, Ana – you’re stupid and clumsy!
穴.reading_hint: Imagine yourself laughing at Ana while you point at her, continuing to jeer as Ana struggles to escape the hole. You’d help Ana out of the hole, but you’re too busy laughing at her.

素.meaning_mnemonic: If you look really close at your <radical>rib cage</radical>, you'll see what looks like millions of tiny little <radical>thread</radical>s. But those aren't threads — they're the essential <kanji>element</kanji>s of life as we know it.
素.meaning_hint: These elements are the threads that tie together every living organism. You can see these elements especially well in large bones like the rib cage, but they're visible in other body parts too.
素.reading_mnemonic: The essential <kanji>element</kanji>s of life need to be kept clean just like everything else, so you make sure to wash them with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ) every single day. This turns out to be pretty hard since the elements are shaped like tiny microscopic threads, but you do your best.
素.reading_hint: Imagine grabbing handfuls of the essential elements of life and trying to wash them with soap. They're slippery so you keep dropping clumps of them, and soap keeps going in your eyes..

省.meaning_mnemonic: You only have a <radical>few</radical> <radical>eye</radical>s so you need to <kanji>conserve</kanji> what's left.
省.meaning_hint: You only have like, three eyes left. If you conserve them.
省.reading_mnemonic: You're trying to <kanji>conserve</kanji> your eyes, when the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) marches by. He stops, sees you, and demands you give him your remaining eyes! But sir, I'm conserving what little I have left!
省.reading_hint: The Shougun stares down at you, eyes blazing, and you hand over all but one of your eyes. Now you really have to conserve, or you'll be blind. Thanks Shougun. I bet he has plenty of eyes, he didn't need yours.

値.meaning_mnemonic: A great <radical>leader</radical> can look <radical>straight</radical> at something and know its <kanji>value</kanji>. That social media site? 44 billion dollars. That job position? 50k a year. A four-day work week? Um… sorry, no, there's no value there. See? Leaders can see straight to the heart of matters to grasp something's true value.
値.meaning_hint: Imagine how terrifying it is when your leader looks straight at YOU. Don't worry, though — you've got more value than you give yourself credit for, and your leader can surely see that.
値.reading_mnemonic: One thing your leader knows has great <kanji>value</kanji> is <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Say goodbye to your days of casually snacking on cheesy treats from the conbini, because your leader's decided the value of cheese is going up 1000%!
値.reading_hint: Imagine the despair you feel walking down the cheese aisle at the store and seeing how one little pack of string cheese now costs the same as your monthly rent. Why did your leader have to spike the value of cheese like this?

護.meaning_mnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> that <radical>flowers</radical> held by a <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> are good to <kanji>defend</kanji> yourself with. Even better than a guard dog they say. People are so confused by the turkey sitting on a stool holding flowers that they just lose their will to fight. This turkey is great to defend yourself and your home.
護.meaning_hint: Picture this turkey holding its flowers while sitting on its stool. Hear it gobbling as it defends everything around it with its mere presence.
護.reading_mnemonic: The only thing this flower-bearing turkey can't <kanji>defend</kanji> against is <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご). Godzilla is pretty much undefeatable, especially for a turkey. The turkey is totally unable to defend anything from the mighty Godzilla.
護.reading_hint: Imagine Godzilla approaching the flower turkey. Hear Godzilla roar as the turkey gobbles in fear before being crushed beneath the massive and scaly foot of Godzilla.

備.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> put <radical>flowers</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical>. That is their <radical>task</radical>. It is to <kanji>provide</kanji> and <kanji>equip</kanji> the cliff with flowers.
備.meaning_hint: Imagine a leader carrying flowers up to the top of a cliff. They are saying "I must provide more flowers to the cliff" as they climb up.
備.reading_mnemonic: Why do they <kanji>provide</kanji> and <kanji>equip</kanji> the cliff with flowers? It's all for the <reading>bee</reading>s (び).
備.reading_hint: "The bees must be equipped with more flowers. I will provide these flowers for the bees!"

型.meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>lantern</radical> on top of a <radical>knife</radical> in the <radical>dirt</radical>. You walk up to take the knife and lantern when you realize it's just a <kanji>model</kanji>. It all looked real from far away, but now that you're up close you can see none of it is real.
型.meaning_hint: Why would anyone make a model that looks like a lantern and a knife sticking into the ground?
型.reading_mnemonic: You touch the <kanji>model</kanji> and see that it's made of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! Someone really into lanterns and knives must have been given this model cake as a birthday present! Though they left it out here so they probably didn't like it.
型.reading_hint: Haven't you seen any of those crazy cakes modeled after things like movies, towns, giants hams, and purses? They're all the rage. Next time you make a cake try to make it look like a model lantern and knife with fake cake dirt and everything.

抜.meaning_mnemonic: Touch your <radical>fingers</radical> to your <radical>friend</radical> to <kanji>pull out</kanji> their friendship. Pull it out with a high five. Pull it out with a back rub. Pull it out with an, "Oh no, there's some lint on your shirt, let me get that for you."
抜.meaning_hint: REMINDER: You are not allowed to touch your friends without their permission. If they say no to you pulling out their friendship, then you don't get to pull out their friendship. End of story.
抜.reading_mnemonic: Your friend looks you in the eye and says, I want you to <kanji>pull out</kanji> my hair. I want my head to be <reading>nu</reading>de (ぬ). Now you have to use your fingers on your friend to pull out their hair and make their head all nude.
抜.reading_hint: REMINDER: If someone asks you to pull out their hair to help them achieve a nude head, you don't have to do it unless you want to! Choices and respect, people!

較.meaning_mnemonic: Your new <radical>car</radical> doesn’t <radical>mix</radical> well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.
較.meaning_hint: Imagine this garage of yours with a sea of white cars. Now there is a new car that really sticks out like a sore thumb. Imagine yourself looking out over your cars and seeing this wacky car practically jumping out at you.
較.reading_mnemonic: What did I say this car looks like? Oh that’s right, a pea<reading>cock</reading> (かく). This car looks like a peacock. It’s the color of a peacock and you’ll look like you’re peacocking when you’re out on the town driving it. It really is such a stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to like, every other car out there.
較.reading_hint: See yourself driving around in this peacock car. Hear the engine purr as strangers jeer and yell at you. This car sure does get a lot of attention, doesn’t it?

段.meaning_mnemonic: Cut this <radical>comb</radical> up with your <radical>weapon</radical> and turn it into the <kanji>steps</kanji> for some <kanji>stairs</kanji>.
段.meaning_hint: When you think about it, a comb is like a ladder. So if you cut all the rungs off with your weapon and put them on the ground, they turn into stairs!
段.reading_mnemonic: You slowly work to create these <kanji>stairs</kanji>, you have to slice up a lot of combs, and at <reading>dawn</reading> (だん) they are done!
段.reading_hint: Imagine standing at the top of the stairs you made yourself, looking down at the entire town below you, as the light of dawn breaks and fills everything before you with light. And now more people can see this beautiful sight if they climb your steps.

痛.meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>sick</radical> <radical>mama</radical> tries to do a <radical>task</radical> she's filled with <kanji>pain</kanji>!
痛.meaning_hint: Sick people shouldn't have to do things, of course they're in pain. You shouldn't let your mama do anything if she's in pain, do it for her!
痛.reading_mnemonic: Your mama's <kanji>pain</kanji> is <reading>too</reading> (つう) much for her to handle, so now you have to do all of her tasks too.
痛.reading_hint: You finish doing your tasks and hers too, and you ask her, "How is the pain now, Mama?" "THE PAIN IS TOO MUCH!" she cries. Oh mama.

巨.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>giant</radical> radical and the <kanji>giant</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Massive!
巨.reading_mnemonic: Just recently, a <kanji>giant</kanji> rampaged through Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ), so now it's even smaller than before. Poor Little Kyoto.
巨.reading_hint: See Little Kyoto after the giant rampaged through it. It looks even smaller and more pathetic than ever. Look at all the smoldering rubble and smell the smoke with your nose.

鑑.meaning_mnemonic: See that <radical>gold</radical> man <radical>oversee</radical>ing everyone? He's a <kanji>model</kanji> human. His goldenness is a sign of how exemplary he is, and he's overseeing all of us flawed, regular folks to try to help us get on his level.
鑑.meaning_hint: Imagine this golden man standing on a raised platform, looking down at you judgmentally with his arms crossed. You mutter curses at him under your breath, but really you're just jealous you're not a model human like him.
鑑.reading_mnemonic: As you look closer at the golden <kanji>model</kanji> human, you realize he's actually Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! No wonder he's covered in gold and overseeing everyone — he's the greatest conqueror in history!
鑑.reading_hint: Hold on a sec — didn't Genghis Khan kill a bunch of people and father like a thousand kids? He doesn't seem like such a model person to you, but, hey, you don't make the rules around here — Genghis Khan does.

均.meaning_mnemonic: You have some <radical>dirt</radical> next to a <radical>pool</radical> that has an extra <radical>drop</radical> of water in it because you need them to be <kanji>equal</kanji>. If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.
均.meaning_hint: Imagine seeing a pool that isn't equal. It's like a tiny kiddy pool with an inch of water. And it's surrounded by mounds of dirt. No no no, this won't do—add more water to make them equal.
均.reading_mnemonic: You spend a few hours making sure the water-to-dirt ratio is perfectly <kanji>equal</kanji>, not for yourself, but because the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England told you to. You may agree that equality is important, but it's the King's responsibility to keep things that way. Which is why he ordered you, small peon, to do the equalizing work for him.
均.reading_hint: Okay, so the existence of a king kind of makes things not, well, equal, for all people. But at least aesthetically everything will be!

探.meaning_mnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> to your <radical>forehead</radical> and stand your <radical>legs</radical> high up in a <radical>tree</radical> if you want to <kanji>look for</kanji> something. After all, shielding your eyes and getting up high is the best way to look for something, isn’t it?
探.meaning_hint: Imagine it's like a quest from a book or game. What are you looking for? Maybe a powerful sword, or a lost princess. Picture it in your mind, this thing you're looking for.
探.reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>look for</kanji> it for so long that you can't help getting a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). I mean, you're up some tree, shielding your eyes from the sun as you look for that thing, and it's been hours, days? Of course you're going to get a tan.
探.reading_hint: Feel the sun's rays on your skin as you search and search and search. Watch as your skin gets darker, crispier, and — hmm — starts smelling like cooked meat. Now that's a good tan.

操.meaning_mnemonic:
If someone has their <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>syrup</radical>, it's just another way of saying they <kanji>manipulate</kanji>d you. Cause only slimy people manipulate other people, so their fingers are slimy and sticky because they're covered in your syrup from mucking up your life.

This also has another meaning: <kanji>chastity</kanji>. Just imagine if someone wanted you to keep your chastity and they called s.e.x. when someone "puts their fingers in your syrup." That would be pretty effective, right? Cause it's like, super gross.
操.meaning_hint: "Ah golly gee wiz, he sure got his fingers all up in my syrup again, dagnabbit." You know, this saying we say all the time when someone manipulates us into doing something.
操.reading_mnemonic: If you want to <kanji>manipulate</kanji> someone else it's going to have a permanent effect on your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Being manipulative isn't cool. And your soul will suffer forever for it.
操.reading_hint: What happens to your soul, you ask? It gets covered in syrup. That's probably where the syrup fingers phrase came from in the first place.

熟.meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>receive</radical> something edible, arrange it in a <radical>circle</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it. That way, it’ll <kanji>ripen</kanji> more quickly.
熟.meaning_hint: Picture yourself receiving something, arranging it in a circle in the pan, and leaving it to boil. See how quickly that stuff ripens? Now it’s all ripe and yummy.
熟.reading_mnemonic: Once everything you received has <kanji>ripen</kanji>ed, you kick back near your <reading>juke</reading>box (じゅく) and eat it. The jukebox sets the mood, making the ripened treat feels twice as sweet.
熟.reading_hint: Imagine the jukebox playing music to accompany your ripened treat. Ahh, jukebox music and ripened goodies. Can life get any sweeter?

縦.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>thread</radical> <radical>loiter</radical>s on the <radical>horns</radical> of a <radical>coat rack</radical>, it's always <kanji>vertical</kanji>. By loitering I mean hanging around, and by horns I mean the little bits sticking off a coat rack that you hang your vertical threads on. Obviously.
縦.meaning_hint: Picture this thread hanging (loitering) on the horn of your coat rack. Imagine yourself putting it there, then observing how it hangs. Naturally it's going to be vertical, right? That's just physics, baby.
縦.reading_mnemonic: Whether or not a thread will hang <kanji>vertical</kanji> depends on how you <reading>立て</reading>る (たて) your coat rack. If you 立てる the coat rack properly, the threads will hang off it in a perfectly vertical way. On the other hand, if you 立てる it carelessly, things might end up kind of kind of diagonal and weird.
縦.reading_hint: Think about how careful you're going to be when you 立てる your coat rack to make sure the stuff hanging from it is as vertical as can be. If things aren't perfectly vertical, you'll dismantle it and 立てる it again till they are.

幕.meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>greenhouse</radical>, a <radical>towel</radical> becomes a <kanji>curtain</kanji> as soon as you hang it. Greenhouses are made of windows, it's all glass, there are no walls. So when you put a towel in there it turns into a curtain. It has no choice but to cover a window, after all.
幕.meaning_hint: Imagine hanging towels over all the windows in the greenhouse. They're really fuzzy, thick curtains now.
幕.reading_mnemonic: You're hanging these towel <kanji>curtain</kanji>s because you need a quiet place to eat a Big <reading>Mac</reading> (まく). But you're a little ashamed. Big Macs aren't really good for you, but this stupid greenhouse has too many windows and everyone can see you stuff your face. So that's where the towel curtains come in.
幕.reading_hint: Imagine hiding in a greenhouse blocked off with towel curtains, stuffing your face with Big Macs. No one can shame you in here. You can eat as many as you want. And hey, you can use the curtains as napkins too.

腐.meaning_mnemonic: Set up your <radical>canopy</radical>, <radical>attach</radical> some <radical>meat</radical> to it, and the meat will eventually <kanji>rot</kanji>.
腐.meaning_hint: Imagine the smell of rotten meat hanging from your canopy. Now no one wants to come near here.
腐.reading_mnemonic: You let meat <kanji>rot</kanji>. You're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). That's really all I can say at this point.
腐.reading_hint: What, did you think attaching this meat to the canopy would make it turn into jerky or something? Fool...

払.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> are <radical>private</radical>, no one can see them unless they <kanji>pay</kanji>! And you decide if what they pay is enough to get a peek.
払.meaning_hint: Imagine people coming from miles around to pay for a look at your private fingers.
払.reading_mnemonic: If someone tries to look without paying, that's <reading>hara</reading>ssment (はら)! Nobody gets to see something of yours without proper payment, but more importantly, your consent!
払.reading_hint: If somebody looks at your fingers without paying, without your consent, file a harassment claim on their butt! That is NOT okay!

壁.meaning_mnemonic: There are <radical>hot peppers</radical> hiding in this <radical>dirt</radical>, so you build a <kanji>wall</kanji> to protect yourself from them. Have you ever stepped on a really hot pepper? The oils bleed into your shoes and burn your feet! You don't want anyone stepping on these so you wall them up.
壁.meaning_hint: Picture yourself walking around and seeing a wall around some dirt. You look inside and there are some hot peppers hiding just below the surface. Phew! Good thing this wall was here to protect you from them.
壁.reading_mnemonic: On the other side of this <kanji>wall</kanji> there are much less threatening <reading>cob</reading>s (かべ) of corn. Corn isn't spicy. It's delicious and sweet, so it's free from being walled in.
壁.reading_hint: Picture some corn cobs. Now picture some hot peppers. Put a wall between them.

群.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>buddy</radical> the <radical>sheep</radical> is part of a <kanji>flock</kanji>. A flock of sheep. Your sheep buddy is a member of a flock.
群.meaning_hint: Imagine following your sheep buddy back to his flock. Feel the soft fur of all the sheep around you as they baa peacefully.
群.reading_mnemonic: Now take out your <reading>gun</reading> (ぐん) and murder every sheep in the <kanji>flock</kanji>. Except for your buddy. You’re incredibly jealous of your buddy and you don’t like the attention his flock gives him so that’s why you decided to murder them all with a gun.
群.reading_hint: Feel the recoil of the gun in your hands and the ringing of the shots in your ears as you slaughter the flock without remorse. BANG BANG BANG!

蜂.meaning_mnemonic: On the <radical>insect</radical> <radical>signpost</radical> there is a single sign that says <kanji>bee</kanji>.
蜂.meaning_hint: This particular insect signpost points you to where the bee is.
蜂.reading_mnemonic: Thanks to the insect signpost you find the <kanji>bee</kanji>. You make it just in time to see the bee <reading>hatch</reading> (はち) from its egg.
蜂.reading_hint: You got to see a single bee hatch! What a beautiful sight. Weird that a signpost points to it.

網.meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>thread</radical>-covered <radical>hill</radical>, but it's all lumpy and weird. You approach it to take a closer look, and you see it's actually a huge <kanji>net</kanji> with a bunch of stuff caught in it: fish, cats, tree branches. All this random stuff is what's making the net look so lumpy!
網.meaning_hint: You'll need to differentiate this from 綱, which uses the same radicals (though 綱 has a real hill radical). Just remember that the "hill" in this one is all weird and lumpy because it's a net with a bunch of stuff caught in it.
網.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>net</kanji> is creating a serious obstacle to you <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) your lawn. The net has way too much stuff in it for you to be able to drag it away, so for now you're just going to have to mow around it.
網.reading_hint: Imagine the awkwardness of trying to mow the grass around this big, lumpy, writhing net full of loudly meowing cats, flopping fish, and goodness knows what else.

匹.meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.
匹.meaning_hint: You can only see the legs, though, because a cloth is covering the cage. What kind of small animal is it? Try to look.
匹.reading_mnemonic: You look up through the cloth, and see a hairless cat. This <kanji>small animal</kanji> is hairless, but most surprisingly is covered in <reading>hickie</reading>s (ひき).
匹.reading_hint: Who is giving this cat all those hickies? Pretty gross, if you ask me.

崖.meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>mountain</radical> on top of a bunch of <radical>dirt</radical> with a steep <radical>cliff</radical>. This is a bit redundant, but the mountain and the dirt just make the <kanji>cliff</kanji> even bigger and steeper and scarier.
崖.meaning_hint: Imagine a cliff. Now add two big helpings of dirt and a mountain to the top. It's still a cliff, it's just more cliff than before.
崖.reading_mnemonic: You can't believe how big the <kanji>cliff</kanji> is now with the mountain and the dirt. You look at it for so long, you just become another <reading>gawker</reading> (がけ) who can't stop staring at the huge cliff. It really is huge.
崖.reading_hint: Imagine a giant cliff that turns every passerby into a gawker, just like you.

蛇.meaning_mnemonic: There is an <radical>insect</radical> on the <radical>roof</radical> in a <radical>spoon</radical>. It's there for the eating, and it's how you plan to attract a <kanji>snake</kanji> to your roof.
蛇.meaning_hint: Snakes love insects, especially when they're ready to eat on a spoon. But, snakes don't usually go on top of the roof, so that's why you have to tempt one with an insect in a spoon.
蛇.reading_mnemonic: Why don't you just bring a <kanji>snake</kanji> up to the roof yourself? Because snakes are <reading>heavy</reading> (へび).
蛇.reading_hint: Especially big snakes. They're really, really hebi.

蓄.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> are <radical>mysterious</radical>ly appearing in the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. No matter what you do to them, they continue to <kanji>amass</kanji>.
蓄.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself spraying poison on these mysterious flowers, but they just double in size, and continue to amass.
蓄.reading_mnemonic: As the flowers <kanji>amass</kanji> it starts attracting wildlife. Soon thousands of baby <reading>chick</reading>s (ちく) are amassing in the rice paddy as well.
蓄.reading_hint: Feel the wetness of the rice paddy under your feet and the fluffiness of the chicks pressing in on you. Watch the looming, mysterious flowers above you as the chicks smother you.

駄.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> that is <radical>fat</radical> is a <kanji>burdensome</kanji> animal.
駄.meaning_hint: A fat horse. So burdensome. Not to mention the burden on the horse on top of the fatness.
駄.reading_mnemonic: But you have to be nice to the <kanji>burdensome</kanji> horse because this horse is your <reading>da</reading>d (だ).
駄.reading_hint: Go visit your dad the burdensome horse because you have to visit your dad. It's weird because you're a human and he's a horse.

膨.meaning_mnemonic: Play your <radical>drum</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical> and your <radical>hair</radical> will <kanji>swell</kanji>! The more you play and the longer it's under the moon, the larger your hair will swell.
膨.meaning_hint: Have you been suffering from flat, boring hair? Well do I have the swelling treatment you've been looking for!
膨.reading_mnemonic: Using this method, your hair <kanji>swell</kanji>s so much it looks like a giant <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball is sitting on top of your head. It's all round, and well... swollen is probably the only word that fits.
膨.reading_hint: Imagine watching your hair swell to the size of a large bowling ball. Imagine never being able to go bowling again because the comparison is too easy for people to make.

忌.meaning_mnemonic: If you only let <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself) into your <radical>heart</radical>, you'll end up <kanji>mourning</kanji> all of the loves and friendships you could have had but didn't let in.
忌.meaning_hint: Picture yourself all alone. You never let anyone into your heart but yourself, and now you're mourning the life and friends you could have had.
忌.reading_mnemonic: While you're in <kanji>mourning</kanji>, you feel a sharp pain in your crying eyes. A <reading>key</reading> (き) comes sliding out with your tears. You look around wildly, but you're still alone. Is this... the key of mourning?
忌.reading_hint: You try to push the key back into your body. Back into your eye, you even try to swallow it, but it comes back out. Finally you look down at your mourning heart and try the key there. It clicks. And opens.

凄.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>ice</radical> <radical>wife</radical> is <kanji>amazing</kanji>. She's also <kanji>terrible</kanji>, because she's very cold and has many ice powers, but that's also part of what makes her amazing!
凄.meaning_hint: Picture your amazing and terrible ice wife. She's floating in your backyard cackling and shooting ice all around.
凄.reading_mnemonic: She turns her <kanji>amazing</kanji> gaze onto your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and hits it with an ice beam. Woah! Instead of turning to ice or shattering, it steals her awful powers. Now your saber is amazing and terrible too!
凄.reading_hint: Now you're floating in the backyard cackling with your amazing wife as you use your terrible saber and freeze things with her!
